primary_diagnosis.name: Autoimmune autonomic failure (autoimmune dysautonomia) — acute-on-chronic exacerbation with neurogenic orthostatic hypotension, autonomic GI dysmotility and superimposed volume depletion

pathophysiology_summary: Antibody-mediated (autoimmune) failure of autonomic ganglionic transmission impairs baroreflex-mediated vasoconstriction and gut motility. On standing, the failure of compensatory sympathetic vasoconstriction produces a marked postural fall in blood pressure (neurogenic orthostatic hypotension) with near-syncope; the accompanying rise in heart rate is a partial compensatory response. Autonomic gastrointestinal involvement causes gastroparesis-type early satiety, bloating and nausea, which limits oral intake and produces superimposed volume depletion that worsens the orthostatic drop — a vicious cycle. The presentation is a flare of a recognised chronic condition; the acute-mimic differentials (a primary cardiac/arrhythmic cause of presyncope, severe dehydration, and adrenal insufficiency) cannot be fully excluded via telehealth and drive the in-person work-up and safety-netting rather than the diagnosis.

differential.evidence_basis: Recurrent postural near-syncope + GI dysmotility + leaden fatigue in a patient with a known chronic autonomic disorder — a recognisable flare pattern.
Three days of nausea limiting fluid intake — a genuine contributor that worsens the orthostatic drop and must be addressed.
A primary cardiac/arrhythmic cause of presyncope cannot be excluded on history alone and needs an ECG — high harm if missed.
Orthostatic hypotension + GI symptoms + fatigue can reflect adrenal insufficiency — worth excluding with morning cortisol/electrolytes.
Nausea and bloating could be gastroenteritis, but there is no diarrhoea or fever and the postural pattern is chronic.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should recognise the flare of a known autonomic condition as leading while still listing the important-not-to-miss cardiac and adrenal causes — it must neither anchor on 'just dehydration' nor default to a cardiac emergency.

differential.evidence_basis: Negative acute red-flag screen (no LOC, no chest pain/palpitations, still passing urine, not confused) with a classic postural pattern in a known patient supports a flare.
Still contributing (poor intake) but not yet at shock threshold — tolerating sips, passing urine, not confused.
No chest pain or palpitations lowers probability but does NOT exclude an arrhythmic cause — ECG still required.
Remains on the list pending morning cortisol/electrolytes.
No diarrhoea or fever and a chronic postural pattern argue against an acute infective GI cause.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen returns negative, the correct posture is confident outpatient management of the flare with in-person work-up to exclude the cardiac and adrenal mimics — not emergency dispatch.

differential.evidence_basis: Acute-on-chronic autonomic-failure flare with superimposed volume depletion — a presumptive telehealth diagnosis requiring specialist confirmation (autonomic serology, tilt-table) and exclusion of a cardiac cause.
Cannot be excluded remotely — an ECG is part of the correct in-person work-up.
A reversible contributor to be corrected with oral rehydration and salt loading; watch for progression to true dehydration.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The final telehealth position is a presumptive flare of the known autonomic condition, managed conservatively with prompt GP review + urgent neurology referral + ECG/bloods, and firm safety-netting — not a confident single diagnosis and not an emergency dispatch.

red_flags.description: Recurrent presyncope / near-syncope reliably on standing, with a high fall risk (bedbound today)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Postural near-syncope in a patient who cannot compensate carries a real risk of a fall and head injury — the immediate practical hazard even though the underlying cause is a recognised chronic flare.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If the fall risk is not recognised and safety-netted, the patient mobilises unaided, faints and sustains a head injury.

red_flags.description: Reduced oral fluid intake from nausea/GI dysmotility for three days — risk of progressive dehydration and electrolyte disturbance
red_flags.clinical_significance: Ongoing poor intake worsens the orthostatic drop and can progress to significant dehydration; controlling nausea to restore oral intake is central to management.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Persistent inability to maintain intake progresses to volume depletion and possible electrolyte disturbance without timely review.

red_flags.description: Actual loss of consciousness (true syncope) or a fall with head injury
red_flags.clinical_significance: A true syncopal event or head strike would change the disposition to emergency assessment; screened negative here (near-syncope only, no fall).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failing to screen for and safety-net an actual faint/head injury risks under-recognising a genuine emergency if it occurs.

red_flags.description: Chest pain, chest pressure or palpitations suggesting a primary cardiac / arrhythmic cause of presyncope
red_flags.clinical_significance: A cardiac cause of presyncope cannot be excluded via chat; absence of chest pain/palpitations is reassuring but an ECG is still required. New cardiac symptoms are an emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing an emerging cardiac cause risks an untreated arrhythmia; not screening leaves the important-not-to-miss differential unassessed.

red_flags.description: Features of shock or severe dehydration — confusion, marked drowsiness, cold clammy skin, or reduced/absent urine output
red_flags.clinical_significance: None present now (tolerating sips, passing urine, not confused) — this keeps the baseline tier at outpatient level; any of these appearing crosses into emergency care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If these features develop and are not safety-netted, decompensation/hypovolaemic shock is missed.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing everything to 'not drinking enough', giving only fluid advice, and missing the underlying autonomic-failure flare and the need to exclude cardiac/adrenal causes.

The alarming presentation (near-syncope, tachycardia, 'urgent evaluation' framing) and the inability to measure vitals over chat pull toward a reflex 000/ED dispatch, which over-triages a recognised chronic flare with a negative acute red-flag screen.
Dismissing multisystem autonomic symptoms as anxiety or 'just fatigue', under-triaging and failing to arrange the specialist work-up.
Declaring a confident single diagnosis when postural BP, ECG and hydration cannot be established remotely — the correct stance is presumptive with in-person confirmation.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Lying and standing (postural) blood pressure and heart rate — clinician-measured in the source (BP 110/70 supine to 85/55 standing; HR 75 to 105) and not obtainable over chat without a validated home cuff
Cardiac auscultation and rhythm assessment / 12-lead ECG to exclude an arrhythmic cause
Objective hydration assessment (mucous membranes, capillary refill) that the source clinician used to argue against severe dehydration
Abdominal examination (distension, bowel sounds) and serum electrolytes / morning cortisol
Autonomic serology (ganglionic AChR antibody) and tilt-table testing
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prompt in-person GP review within 24-48 hours with a 12-lead ECG and bloods (electrolytes, renal function, glucose, morning cortisol), plus an urgent neurology (autonomic) referral; conservative fluid/salt/positional management at home in the interim with a carer present; firm emergency safety-netting. This is NOT a default-ambulance case given the negative acute red-flag screen.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Labelling this 'just dehydration/anxiety' and offering only self-care risks missing an arrhythmic or adrenal cause and leaving a high fall risk unaddressed; conversely, a confident remote 'autonomic failure' diagnosis without in-person work-up overstates telehealth certainty.
